ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic taco class; no fish-taco-specific subclass exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKW covers taco as a bread-based dish; no fish-taco-specific code exists in the published FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept for fish tacos identified in the published SNOMED CT international release.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of fish tacos consists of 2 tacos, each made with one corn tortilla shell filled with seasoned fish, shredded lettuce, diced tomatoes, onions, and sour cream, weighing approximately 200 g in total.

nutrient_source.title: Fish Tacos — Singapore Health Promotion Board Foodo Database (HPB)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Fish Tacos (cr_id F05020043), confidence 0.95. All seven reported nutrients (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre) are preserved from the canonical HPB record. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central reference for a comparable fish taco composite (USDA SR Legacy item 'Taco with fish, corn tortilla') as HPB does not report saturated fat separately for this item.

gi_source.title: Corn tortilla GI reference — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for fish tacos as a composite dish exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 46 (low) based on the dominant carbohydrate vehicle: corn tortilla (GI ~46, per Atkinson et al. 2008 International Tables). The fish, lettuce, tomato, onion, and sour cream components contribute negligible additional carbohydrate and do not materially alter the GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (46 × 17.05 × 2.0) / 100 = 15.7 g GL for a 200 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 330 mg per 100 g; a 200 g serving delivers approximately 660 mg sodium, representing 29% of the 2,300 mg daily limit.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should choose grilled rather than battered fish to avoid additional sodium from seasoned coatings, request reduced sour cream, and avoid adding high-sodium condiments such as hot sauce or soy sauce, keeping total serving sodium below 600 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate 17.05 g per 100 g with a low estimated GI of 46 (corn tortilla basis); dietary fibre 2.37 g and protein 11.32 g per 100 g moderate the postprandial glucose response.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with type 2 diabetes should use corn tortillas rather than white flour tortillas, choose grilled fish over battered fish to avoid refined carbohydrate from the coating, and limit to 2 tacos per meal to keep total carbohydrate load within a single-meal target of approximately 45–60 g.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat 11.8 g per 100 g including estimated saturated fat 3.1 g and reported trans fat 0.1 g per 100 g; sodium 330 mg per 100 g is also a concern for cardiovascular risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should select grilled white fish (rich in lean protein and potential omega-3) over battered or fried variants, request minimal sour cream to reduce saturated fat intake, and avoid high-sodium condiments to keep the dish within a heart-healthy dietary pattern.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Trans fat 0.1 g per 100 g is pro-inflammatory; grilled fish provides potential omega-3 anti-inflammatory benefit; nightshade ingredients (tomato) may trigger symptoms in sensitive individuals.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should choose grilled fish to maximise omega-3 benefit and minimise AGE formation, avoid battered variants, monitor nightshade tolerance (tomato-based salsa), and request reduced sour cream to lower saturated fat.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fish tacos are a Mexican dish originating from the Baja California peninsula, consisting of corn tortilla shells filled with seasoned grilled or battered white fish (commonly cod, tilapia, or mahi-mahi), shredded lettuce or cabbage, diced tomatoes, diced onions, and sour cream. The dish delivers a balance of textures — the soft corn tortilla, flaky fish, crisp vegetables, and creamy sour cream — with a mild, savoury flavour profile that can be enhanced with lime juice, salsa, or hot sauce. Per 100 g, fish tacos provide 224 kcal, 11.32 g protein, 11.8 g fat, and 17.05 g carbohydrate, with 2.37 g dietary fibre and 330 mg sodium. The corn tortilla base has a low estimated glycemic index of approximately 46, making the dish moderately suitable for blood glucose management when grilled fish is chosen. The sodium content of 330 mg per 100 g places it in the borderline range for hypertension management, and the estimated saturated fat of 3.1 g per 100 g warrants attention for cardiovascular health. Fish tacos are widely consumed as a lunch or dinner item and are popular in Mexican, Tex-Mex, and Californian cuisine contexts. They are not suitable for vegans or vegetarians and may contain gluten if wheat tortillas are substituted.
common_user_queries: How many calories are in fish tacos?
Are fish tacos good for diabetics?
What is the GI of fish tacos?
How much protein is in fish tacos?
Are fish tacos high in sodium?
Can I eat fish tacos if I have high blood pressure?
What fish is used in fish tacos?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrients (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre) sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database entry for Fish Tacos (cr_id F05020043) with a reported confidence of 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA SR Legacy closest composite match and documented. GI estimated from peer-reviewed International Tables of Glycemic Index (Atkinson et al. 2008) using corn tortilla as the dominant carbohydrate vehicle, with estimation basis fully documented.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (3.1 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy reference for a comparable fish taco composite, as HPB does not report saturated fat separately for this item. This value should be replaced with a direct HPB measurement if one becomes available. 2. Trans fat (0.1 g/100 g) is carried forward from the input record caution note; this value is not explicitly reported in the HPB source and should be verified. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 because detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient, and moisture data for this composite fish taco preparation are not reported in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as a matched composite entry. 4. GI value of 46 is an analogous estimate based on corn tortilla GI from Atkinson et al. 2008 International Tables; no direct GI measurement for fish tacos as a composite dish exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 5. Fish tacos are a Mexican dish; multilingual naming rules for Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, and Vietnamese cuisines do not apply. Spanish romanized form 'tacos de pescado' is provided as the culturally relevant local-language alias. 6. The input record listed occasion_tags: ['breakfast'] which is atypical for fish tacos; this has been corrected to lunch/dinner in meal_context. 7. The input health_suitability entry for rheumatoid_arthritis listed suitable: false but the reason text described positive attributes — this logical inconsistency has been resolved in additional_conditions with a balanced moderate concern assessment.
